item.title: Net zero is not a zero-sum game
item.description: Fresh polling shows a new divide on the right but support remains high for UK climate policy

item.title: Unilever in talks to sell food division to spice and sauce maker McCormick
item.description: Consumer goods giant pivots to beauty and home care

item.title: EU agrees on review of emissions trading system as energy crisis bites
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Macron urges Brussels to counter election interference

item.title: Big Brother is watching at JPMorgan
item.description: The bank is deploying technology to ensure junior employees are not under-reporting working hours

item.title: Could the US really release more of its strategic oil reserves?
item.description: Desperation, thy name is Scott Bessent

item.title: The case for slicing up consumer conglomerates further
item.description: Investors are better able to value pure plays, while lacklustre units can dilute overall growth and margins at sprawling companies

item.title: Edward Jones insists AI will not replace its $2.5tn financial adviser network
item.description: Clients will want human insights even as new technology shakes up investing, according to chief executive Penny Pennington

item.title: Will Saturday Night Live work in the UK?
item.description: Sky is placing a multimillion-pound bet on remaking the long-running US comedy show for a British audience

item.title: The war over tail risks is in full swing
item.description: Trump’s financial fight with insurers will affect business confidence as much as the kinetic battle

item.title: Airlines draw up contingency plans over jet fuel shortage fears
item.description: War in the Middle East is disrupting supplies and driving up prices

item.title: Maritime security is key to global energy supplies
item.description: Companies need to know their vessels can pass through busy shipping lanes repeatedly and safely

item.title: Military briefing: the perils of the Hormuz escort plan
item.description: Mines, fast boats and missiles would threaten one of the most dangerous US naval missions in decades

item.title: JPMorgan deploys tech to monitor junior bankers’ working hours
item.description: Tool checks staff are not under-reporting time they spend working

item.title: How feeder funds fuel insurers’ private credit binge
item.description: Rated note feeders attract favourable capital treatment, transforming stakes in private markets funds into top-rated bonds

item.title: AI sell-off’s worst-hit company grapples with existential threat
item.description: Shares of Australian software maker Atlassian have fallen by more than half as Nasdaq 100 group cuts 10% of workforce

item.title: Introducing Untold: Opus Dei
item.description: Introducing Opus Dei, a new season of Untold from the Financial Times.

item.title: The ‘Armageddon scenario’ for gas markets
item.description: Traders and analysts warn of lasting disruption after damage to Qatar facility that supplies a fifth of the world’s LNG

item.title: China’s crackdown on fuel and fertiliser exports spurs supply fears
item.description: Beijing is trying to preserve its own stockpiles by limiting sales

item.title: Adani’s win against Vedanta in takeover fight leaves shareholders with nothing
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Indian graduates struggle to find jobs

item.title: Supermicro co-founder charged in conspiracy to export Nvidia chips to China
item.description: US prosecutors allege two employees and a contractor smuggled servers through south-east Asia

item.title: FirstFT: Donald Trump praises Japan’s ‘tremendous support’ over Iran war
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: the ‘Armageddon scenario’ for gas markets, and China moves to cool Hong Kong IPO boom

item.title: China curbs ‘low-quality’ listings to cool Hong Kong IPO boom
item.description: Regulator targets some opaque ‘red-chip’ structures but signals the market for new issues is still open

item.title: Deutsche Bank promotes two potential successors to Sewing
item.description: Stefan Hoops, chief executive of DWS, to join bank’s management board and Fabrizio Campelli appointed deputy CEO

item.title: The Fed’s juggling act
item.description: How will the US central bank handle inflation and a slowing economy?

item.title: Gilts sell off after Bank of England warns of inflation risks
item.description: MPC holds rates at 3.75% as war in Middle East continues to push energy costs higher

item.title: SEC urged to restrict Chinese companies’ access to US capital markets
item.description: Fear of national security risks and investor protections spurs rare bipartisan call for action from financial regulator

item.title: Spanish defence group halts merger in blow to Daniel Loeb
item.description: Indra, a radar and sensor specialist, abandons proposed takeover of weapons maker EM&E over conflict of interest concerns

item.title: IG Group considers New York listing in latest blow to London’s stock exchange
item.description: Online trading group said in a review it would also consider acquisitions

item.title: This gas crisis is bad — but not as bad as the last one
item.description: Whatever happens in the Middle East, European prices are unlikely to rise as much as they did in 2022

item.title: Investors’ important bet on nuclear fusion
item.description: The elusive power source is gradually moving closer to becoming a reality
